name: Tsukuyomi
shortDescription: Tsukuyomi is the moon god in Japanese mythology, born from Izanagi's right eye. He is brother to Amaterasu (sun) and Susanoo (storm).
description: Tsukuyomi, also known as Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto, is the moon god in Japanese mythology. Born from Izanagi's right eye as he purified himself after visiting Yomi, the underworld, Tsukuyomi is one of the three noble children, alongside Amaterasu (the sun goddess, born from Izanagi's left eye) and Susanoo (the storm god, born from Izanagi's nose). While Amaterasu is prominently featured, Tsukuyomi's role is less defined in primary sources like the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki. He is often associated with the night, the moon, and the lunar cycle. A key myth involves Tsukuyomi killing Ukemochi, the food goddess, out of disgust at her method of creating food, which led to Amaterasu's anger and the separation of the sun and moon, explaining why they never appear together. Despite his significant birth, Tsukuyomi's stories are fewer and his character less developed than his siblings.
subtitle: The enigmatic ruler of the night.
achievements: Born from Izanagi's right eye, marking him as divine.
Ruled the night sky and governed the moon.
Caused the separation of the sun and moon due to his actions.
quests: To govern the night and maintain the lunar cycle.
To uphold his divine responsibilities as one of the three noble children.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Tsukuyomi's birth is detailed in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki. After Izanagi's harrowing visit to Yomi, he underwent purification rites. As he washed his right eye, Tsukuyomi was born. He was then tasked with ruling the night sky. The most prominent myth featuring Tsukuyomi involves Ukemochi, the food goddess. When Ukemochi produced food from her nose, mouth, and other bodily orifices, Tsukuyomi was disgusted and killed her. This act angered Amaterasu, who refused to ever face Tsukuyomi again, resulting in the separation of day and night.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Tsukuyomi's primary role is to govern the night and the moon. He represents the lunar cycle, darkness, and the passage of time. While not as extensively worshipped as Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi held a significant position in the Shinto pantheon. His association with the moon connects him to themes of mystery, reflection, and the subconscious. The separation of Amaterasu and Tsukuyomi is a foundational myth explaining the alternation of day and night, a fundamental aspect of the natural world.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Tsukuyomi symbolizes the moon, night, and the cyclical nature of time. His reserved nature and limited stories suggest a more introspective and mysterious aspect of the divine. The act of killing Ukemochi can be interpreted as a conflict between purity and necessity, or a rejection of certain aspects of the natural world. The moon itself often symbolizes the subconscious, intuition, and the feminine principle, contrasting with the sun's association with consciousness, reason, and the masculine.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tsukuyomi's legacy persists in Shinto shrines and rituals, though he is not as widely venerated as Amaterasu or Susanoo. He continues to be recognized as the god of the moon and night. In modern Japanese culture, Tsukuyomi appears in various forms of media, including anime, manga, and video games, often portrayed as a powerful and enigmatic figure. His story continues to inspire artistic interpretations and reflects the enduring fascination with the moon and its mysteries.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, one of the oldest chronicles of Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another primary source for Japanese mythology.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: Tsukuyomi is the god of the moon.

companions.name: Amaterasu
companions.description: The sun goddess, born from Izanagi's left eye.

companions.name: Susanoo
companions.description: The storm god, born from Izanagi's nose.

worshipPractices.name: Shrine Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of rice, sake, and other symbolic items are made at shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi.

festivals.name: Tsukimi (Moon Viewing Festival)
festivals.description: A traditional Japanese festival celebrating the harvest moon, often associated with lunar deities.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tsukuyomi's name and image are used in modern fiction, games, and anime, often representing lunar power and mystery. He is a symbol of Japanese mythology and cultural heritage.
